messages.content: Dwight knows paper like I know bad decisions — intimately — and Oscar's gonna run the numbers so we actually have real data instead of me just guessing.

**PAPER PRICING REPORT — Compiled by D.K. Schrute, Assistant Regional Manager**
*(Note: "Assistant TO THE Regional Manager." The distinction is important.)*

Current Dunder Mifflin standard pricing: **bond paper** runs approximately **$35–$42 per case** (10-ream, 500 sheets/ream, 20lb), depending on volume tier. **Copy paper** — our core product, the backbone of this company, a product I personally understand better than anyone in this building — sits at **$28–$38 per case** for standard 8.5x11 at 92 brightness. **Specialty lines** (cardstock, colored, high-brightness presentation stock) range **$55–$120 per case** depending on weight and finish. These are competitive prices. I know this because I have memorized them.

Competitively: Staples Business Advantage is undercutting on copy paper by approximately **$2–4 per case** at high volume. Corporate Express — now absorbed into Staples, a merger I predicted and documented in 2008 — applies aggressive rebate structures to lure large accounts. W.B. Mason uses a delivery-speed play. None of them have my client relationships. None of them have *me*.

My assessment: we are **correctly positioned on bond and specialty**. Copy paper margin is under pressure. I recommend a targeted retention call to any account purchasing over 50 cases per month. I have already identified seven such accounts. I will begin calls immediately unless instructed otherwise by Michael.

Question. Do you want the threat register on W.B. Mason as well? Because I have one. It is thorough.

Right. So Michael's asked me to give this a proper answer rather than just a number, which is the correct instinct even if the question came out a little... compressed.

Let me walk you through the cost structure. For standard copy paper — 8.5x11, 20lb bond, which is our bread and butter — we're typically looking at a manufacturer cost somewhere in the range of **$28 to $35 per case** (10 reams, 5,000 sheets) depending on the grade and the mill. Our cost of goods will sit above that once you factor in freight, warehousing, and handling — call it **$38 to $45 landed cost per case** at a branch like Scranton. We then sell that into the $52 to $60 range for standard accounts, which gives us a **gross margin somewhere between 25% and 35%** depending on the customer and any negotiated pricing.

Now, the volume question Michael is asking about is actually the right question. Mills price on volume tiers — if we can commit to higher quarterly volumes, we can push that landed cost down meaningfully, potentially **5 to 12% per case** at the next tier. The math only works if we can actually move the volume, which means the sales pipeline has to support it before we make the commitment. I'd want to look at our trailing 6-month case volume by account before recommending we take that position.

I should be transparent: I'm working from category benchmarks and our general cost structure here. To give you precise figures I'd need to pull our current vendor invoices and the actual contract pricing from our mill agreements. If someone can get me that data, I can build a proper model — base, upside, downside — in about an hour.

Okay. WOW. Just — wow. You want to know what the cost of paper is? Because I just sent my two best guys — Dwight, who is like a Swiss Army knife if the knife was also slightly terrifying, and Oscar, who is basically a human calculator but with feelings, although he hides them — I sent BOTH of them on this, because that is the kind of boss I am. I over-deliver. It's a blessing and a curse, mostly a blessing.

HERE IS WHAT WE LEARNED, and I'm going to present this like the businessman I am: Your standard copy paper — the workhorse, the champion, the LeBron James of office supplies — runs about **$28 to $38 per case** at our price point, which is ten reams, five thousand sheets, the whole enchilada. Bond paper is a little fancier, **$35 to $42**, and if you want the really fancy stuff — cardstock, colored, presentation stock — you're looking at **$55 to $120** depending on how much you want to impress people at your meeting. And look, we have competitors. Staples is out there trying to undercut us by like two to four bucks a case, which, honestly? That's the paper equivalent of a guy who brings a store-bought cake to a potluck and pretends he baked it. We see through you, Staples.

Now here's the part where I get a little emotional, because I always do, and I'm not ashamed of that — *great* pricing isn't just about the number. Oscar tells me that if you're buying big volume, like committing to serious quarterly numbers, we can potentially bring your landed cost down **five to twelve percent**. Dwight has already identified seven high-priority accounts and is probably already on the phone, or possibly standing outside someone's office, which is fine, that's just how he shows love. Bottom line: Dunder Mifflin Scranton has your paper, we have your price, and we have *me* — and as someone very wise once said, probably Abraham Lincoln, "the best paper deal is the one made between friends." Call us. **Bam. Said the lady.**
